metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin a discriminating structural marker in the convergence visualization AC: specify the HTML/SVG element or data-attribute that distinguishes bar-form (e.g., `<rect data-viz='bar'>`) from line-graph form (e.g., `<polyline data-viz='line'>` or `<path data-viz='line'>`), and require the oracle to assert that marker's presence/absence for each test case.
findings.finding: The convergence visualization AC specifies 'asserting the correct form' without pinning what structural HTML/SVG property distinguishes the ≤3-round bar form from the ≥4-round line-graph form. An executor writing the test has no concrete attribute, element type, or CSS class to assert on, so the oracle could be satisfied by a no-op or by asserting only the data table, leaving the visual-form branch untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — convergence visualization AC: 'asserting the correct form and the hidden table contents'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `data-viz-form` attribute (e.g., `data-viz-form="bar"` / `data-viz-form="line"`) to the visualization container element in the template spec, and update the AC oracle to: 'assert the container element has `data-viz-form="bar"` and contains at least one `<rect>` for the 2-round test; assert `data-viz-form="line"` and at least one `<polyline>` or `<path>` for the 4-round test, in addition to asserting the hidden table.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'no JS' / 'collapse all findings' contradiction: either (a) drop the 'collapse all' control (users can close individual <details>) and remove it from the What section, or (b) explicitly carve out a minimal progressive-enhancement JS fallback for this one control while keeping the rest no-JS-required, and update the Approach rationale accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan simultaneously requires 'no JS' (native <details>/<summary> only) and specifies 'a collapse all findings control per round.' These two constraints are mechanically incompatible: there is no browser-native way to imperatively collapse multiple open <details> elements without JavaScript. The control either must be JS-only (contradicting the no-JS constraint) or removed from the spec.
findings.location: What section — 'a collapse all findings control per round' bullet; Approach rationale — 'Native <details>/<summary> gives no-JS collapsibles'
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the 'collapse all findings' control from the What section (individual <details> toggle is sufficient for the auditor use case without a bulk-close) and remove the bullet so the no-JS constraint holds cleanly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'collapse all findings' vs no-JS contradiction: either remove 'collapse all' (it has no AC oracle), accept a small inline `<script>` snippet for it (and say so explicitly), or replace it with a pure-HTML substitute (e.g., a link to reload with all groups collapsed via a query param).
findings.finding: The plan simultaneously mandates 'no JS' (native `<details>` with 'no external JS/asset') and a 'collapse all findings control per round.' Collapsing multiple independent `<details>` elements simultaneously requires JavaScript — there is no pure-HTML mechanism to imperatively set the `open` attribute on a set of elements. This is an unresolved design contradiction: either the 'collapse all' control must be dropped, implemented with a small JS snippet (violating the stated constraint), or worked around with a fragile form/anchor hack.
findings.location: What section — 'a collapse all findings control per round' and Approach rationale — 'no-JS collapsibles with built-in a11y'; Scope — 'inline CSS; the convergence visualization + threshold meter as inline SVG/CSS (no external JS/asset)'
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the 'collapse all findings' control (it has no corresponding AC oracle test) to preserve the no-JS constraint cleanly; if it is desired, add an explicit note that it requires a small inline event-listener script and exclude it from the 'no external JS/asset' constraint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how the round stepper works without JavaScript — specify whether it uses URL query parameters (server re-render per click), in-page anchor links to pre-rendered round sections, or another no-JS mechanism — and ensure 'newest default' is achievable with the chosen approach.
findings.finding: The plan declares a 'no JS' constraint for all interactivity, yet describes a stateful `◂ round n of N ▸` round-navigation stepper with 'newest default' — without specifying how this stepper functions without JavaScript (URL parameter + full server re-render, anchor-link-per-round, or all rounds rendered simultaneously with `<details>`). These two choices lead to substantially different implementations.
findings.location: What section (round stepper description) vs. Approach rationale ('no JS; keyboard + screen-reader support for free')
findings.suggested_fix: Use URL query parameter `?round=N` so each stepper click is a GET request; the server renders only the selected round's findings; `newest default` is the absence of the parameter. The `<a>` links in the stepper emit the correct URLs. No JS required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the round-stepper mechanism explicitly: either (a) clarify that all rounds are always rendered and the stepper is pure in-page anchor navigation (remove 'newest default' or redefine it as 'scroll to newest round anchor on load'), or (b) explicitly allow a minimal JS enhancement for round-switching and update the no-JS clause to 'JS-free for all content; progressive JS for round navigation.'
findings.finding: The round navigation stepper ('◂ round n of N ▸' in a sticky section header, 'newest default') implies that only one round's findings are shown at a time (the others are hidden/shown on click). Dynamically hiding/revealing round sections requires JavaScript; the plan's 'no JS' constraint makes this mechanism impossible unless all rounds are rendered simultaneously and the stepper is reduced to pure in-page anchor navigation — which the 'newest default' language contradicts (if all rounds are visible, there is no meaningful 'default' round to select). The plan does not resolve this tension.
findings.location: What section — 'Round navigation as a ◂ round n of N ▸ stepper (newest default), in a sticky section header'; Approach rationale — 'Native <details>/<summary> gives no-JS collapsibles'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'partial record fallback' sub-section to Empty/partial states: define the rendering behavior when `block_threshold` is absent on a finding (e.g. omit tick, show caption) and when `criteria[]` is absent on a completion verdict (e.g. fall back to the failures-only `findings[]` display with a 'full criterion list unavailable' notice).
findings.finding: The plan specifies no fallback for when the `block_threshold` field is absent on a finding record (old sidecars predating stories A/B), and no fallback for when the `criteria[]` field is absent on a completion verdict (pre-story-C records). The 'Empty/partial states' section covers only absent gate results (not-yet-run panels), not structurally partial records within a present gate result. An implementer has no defined behavior for these degraded sub-step cases.
findings.location: What > Empty/partial states section; What > threshold display section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the sort direction for priority within each decision group explicitly in both the What section and AC #5 (e.g., 'descending by priority — highest priority score first').
findings.finding: The plan and the corresponding AC both state findings are 'sorted by priority within group' but neither specifies the sort direction (ascending vs descending). An executor must guess the direction, and the oracle ('asserting … sorted by priority within group') cannot deterministically pass or fail without knowing which ordering is expected.
findings.location: What section — 'sorted by priority within group'; AC #5 — 'asserting group order, expanded blocking rows, and correct collapsed counts'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, change 'sorted by priority within group' to 'sorted descending by priority within group (highest `priority` score first)'. Update AC #5 oracle to: '… and that within the Blocking group, findings appear in descending priority order (e.g., a finding with priority 0.9 precedes one with priority 0.4).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit error-handling to the `/ticket/<id>` route: return a human-readable 404 when the ticket ID is not found in the read layer, and a 500/error panel (with a logged message) when the read layer raises — document both paths in an AC or in the Scope section.
findings.finding: The `/ticket/<id>` route has no stated error handling for route-level failures: a missing or invalid ticket ID, a read-layer I/O error, or a malformed record. The plan addresses absent-gate data ("not yet run" panels) but is silent on what the server returns when the ticket itself does not exist or when the read layer throws.
findings.location: Scope section (`src/rebar/audit/server.py`); Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers error responses); Compatibility & rollback section
findings.suggested_fix: In `server.py`, wrap the read-layer call in a try/except: raise a Flask/Starlette 404 HTTPException when the ticket record is absent, and catch other I/O errors to return a rendered error template with a logged traceback. Add one AC: 'Requesting /ticket/<unknown-id> returns an HTTP 404 with an explicit "ticket not found" message; a read-layer I/O error returns a 500 error page without a bare traceback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit error-state spec and AC oracle for the case where `/ticket/<id>` receives a ticket ID that does not exist in the read layer (expected: a clear, human-readable 404/not-found panel rather than an exception or blank page).
findings.finding: The plan specifies handling for absent gate data and missing attestations, but does not define the error state when `/ticket/<id>` is called with a non-existent or invalid ticket ID (no 404 handling, no error page spec, no AC oracle for this path).
findings.location: Scope section (`src/rebar/audit/server.py` route) and Acceptance Criteria (all AC items assume a valid ticket record exists)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC item: 'When `/ticket/<id>` is requested for an unknown ticket ID, the server returns a 404 response and renders a human-readable "ticket not found" panel. Oracle: an E2E test asserting a non-existent ID returns HTTP 404 and the response HTML contains the not-found message.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding E2E test) asserting that a request to `/ticket/<unknown-id>` returns a clear HTTP 404 response (or equivalent explicit error page), not an unhandled exception or blank.
findings.finding: The new `/ticket/<id>` HTTP route has no acceptance criterion or test covering a non-existent or invalid ticket ID (e.g., a 404 response). This is a new user-facing flow and the invalid/missing-resource path is unspecified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — no AC covers a missing/invalid ticket ID; Scope: `src/rebar/audit/server.py` (new route)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Oracle: an E2E test requesting `/ticket/nonexistent-id` asserts an HTTP 404 status and an explicit human-readable error message in the response body.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Validate the inline SVG line graph capability assertion by either (a) citing an established server-side SVG chart pattern this codebase or its dependencies already support, (b) scoping it down to a pure CSS bar/sparkline approach (which reflows naturally) for all round counts, or (c) acknowledging the LOC and WCAG-reflow risk and adding an explicit spike/prototype AC before committing to this form.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that a multi-series line graph (blocking as prominent series, advisory/dropped/indeterminate muted) for ≥4 rounds can be produced as 'inline SVG/CSS (no external JS/asset)' within the < 500 LOC per-file policy. This is an unverified capability assertion: building a correctly scaled, labeled, accessible SVG line graph entirely in server-side Jinja2 template code (with a WCAG 1.1.1 visually-hidden data table) is a non-trivial implementation pattern with no prior art in this codebase. The plan does not reference any established server-side SVG generation approach nor validate whether the output is viewport-responsive under 200% zoom (WCAG 1.4.10 reflow).
findings.location: What section — 'for ≥4 rounds, a line graph with BLOCKING as the prominent series'; Approach rationale — 'the convergence visualization + threshold meter as inline SVG/CSS (no external JS/asset)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion for the round stepper: assert it is present on a multi-round ticket, that the newest round is shown by default, and that navigating to an earlier round renders that round's findings.
findings.finding: The round-navigation stepper (`◂ round n of N ▸` with 'newest default') is a described, user-facing deliverable with no covering acceptance criterion. No AC asserts that the stepper is present, navigates correctly, or defaults to the newest round.
findings.location: What section (round stepper description); Acceptance Criteria section (no matching AC)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion for trust signals: assert that a round header displays gate/model version and timestamp, and that the certified badge appears when (and only when) the record is signed.
findings.finding: Trust signals (gate/model version, timestamp, certified badge) are described as a deliverable in the body but have no covering acceptance criterion.
findings.location: What section ('Trust signals in each round header…'); Acceptance Criteria section (no matching AC)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a contrast commitment to the visual system and an AC (or static-analysis tool reference) asserting WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratios (≥4.5:1 for body text, ≥3:1 for large text and UI components) for the verdict/decision chip colors and the grey text palette.
findings.finding: The visual system specifies saturated colors for verdict/decision chips and neutral grey for everything else, but nowhere in the plan or ACs is a WCAG AA contrast ratio (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text/UI components) committed to or verified. 'Inline CSS' authored at implementation time with no contrast assertion could produce inaccessible color combinations.
findings.location: Visual system subsection; Scope ('inline CSS'); Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers contrast)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the visual system: 'All color pairs SHALL meet WCAG 2.1 AA (1.4.3 / 1.4.11). Token values are chosen at design time and verified by axe-core or a contrast-lint step in CI.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a planned update to an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc capturing the finding-centric IA choice (vs. pass-as-navigation) and the neutral threshold meter design, with a pointer to the HCD critique that drove them.
findings.finding: The novel finding-centric IA (finding = row, four passes = in-place provenance, replacing pass-as-navigation) and the neutral threshold meter design are significant architectural decisions whose rationale lives only in the plan ticket, with no update to an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or persistent design doc planned.
findings.location: Approach rationale section; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Update `docs/adr/NNN-finding-centric-audit-ia.md` (or CLAUDE.md) capturing: (1) finding-centric IA rationale vs pass-as-navigation; (2) neutral threshold meter rationale vs signed delta; (3) fixed section order rationale.' Add a corresponding AC: 'An ADR or CLAUDE.md entry is committed alongside the templates.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the ≤3/≥4 convergence-visualization breakpoint into a named constant (e.g., a Jinja2 context variable or server-side config value) so the threshold has a single authoritative location.
findings.finding: The convergence-visualization breakpoint (≤3 rounds → stacked bars; ≥4 rounds → line graph) is a display heuristic hardcoded into the template logic with no configurable seam, making future adjustment a template edit with no single authoritative location.
findings.location: What section — convergence visualization paragraph; Scope — templates
findings.suggested_fix: In `server.py`, define `CONVERGENCE_BAR_MAX_ROUNDS = 3` and pass it to the template context; reference it in the template as `{% if rounds|length <= convergence_bar_max_rounds %}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC #6 to include a 3-round ticket (asserting the bars form, not the line graph) and a 1-round ticket (asserting graceful rendering), covering the branch boundary and degenerate edge of the convergence visualization.
findings.finding: The convergence visualization's branch boundary (exactly 3 rounds = bars; exactly 1 round = bars) is not covered by any AC. The ACs test a 2-round ticket and a 4-round ticket, leaving the boundary value (3 rounds, the last case that uses bars) and the degenerate edge (1 round, where convergence is meaningless) untested.
findings.location: AC: 'The convergence visualization renders stacked per-round bars for a ≤3-round ticket and a blocking-prominent line graph for a ≥4-round ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC #6: 'Also: a 3-round ticket asserts the stacked-bars form (not the line graph), confirming the ≤3 boundary is inclusive; and a 1-round ticket asserts a valid (non-empty, non-error) rendering of the single-bar form.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add stories A/B (plan-review v2 sidecar and code-review v2 sidecar that persist `block_threshold` per finding) to the plan's formal `depends_on`, and verify those stories' sidecar `_slim()`/`build_payload` schemas are updated to include `block_threshold` before this story's threshold-meter AC is written.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the value Pass-3 actually used is persisted PER FINDING by stories A/B (the plan-/code-review v2 sidecars), so the server reads it directly off the finding record — it does NOT do a runtime `criteria_routing` lookup.' But (a) `pass3_decide` in `decide.py` returns only `{decision, reason, validity, impact, priority, severity}` — `block_threshold` is never included in the returned dict and never stamped onto findings by `pass3_over_findings`; (b) the sidecar `_slim()` function in `plan_review/sidecar.py` (lines 308-351) and `code_review/sidecar.py` `build_payload` (lines 85-101) confirm `block_threshold` is not persisted per finding today. The plan names 'stories A/B' as the source of this persistence, but the plan's `depends_on` clause only names 'server-scaffolding story' — stories A/B appear nowhere in the formal dependency list. If this story ships before stories A/B persist `block_threshold`, the threshold meter's tick-mark cannot be read from the finding record, silently breaking or forcing a fallback runtime `criteria_routing` lookup that the plan explicitly disavows.
findings.location: What section — threshold display paragraph ('The value Pass-3 actually used is persisted PER FINDING by stories A/B'); Scope section — `src/rebar/audit/server.py`; AC line for threshold meter
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's 'Compatibility & rollback' or a new 'Dependencies' subsection: 'This story depends on stories A and B having extended the plan-review and code-review sidecar schemas to persist `block_threshold` per finding. The threshold-meter AC (AC line 4) must be blocked until those sidecars ship.' Also add a `depends_on: [story-A-id, story-B-id]` to the ticket metadata.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add stories A/B (the plan-/code-review v2 sidecars that persist `block_threshold` per-finding) as a formal `depends_on` of this story, OR — if the sidecar update is in-scope for this story — specify in the Scope section that `sidecar.py`'s `_slim()` and `pass3_over_findings` must be updated to stamp and persist `block_threshold` per finding before the read layer serves it to the template.
findings.finding: The plan states the server reads `block_threshold` 'directly off the finding record — it does NOT do a runtime `criteria_routing` lookup,' attributing persistence to 'stories A/B (the plan-/code-review v2 sidecars).' In the current codebase, `block_threshold` is NOT persisted into finding records: `pass3_over_findings` computes it locally in the loop and never stamps it into the returned dict, and `sidecar.py`'s `_slim()` does not include it. If stories A/B are a prerequisite for this story, the plan must declare them as an explicit `depends_on`, or, if A/B are part of the same epic, the plan must specify what the read layer's finding schema must carry. Without this, the threshold-meter mechanism has a hidden ordering dependency that silently breaks (the meter tick renders at 0 or a fallback rather than the per-finding value) if this story ships before A/B.
findings.location: What section — 'Threshold display = a neutral meter PER FINDING' paragraph; specifically 'the server reads it directly off the finding record… persisted PER FINDING by stories A/B'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact field name (e.g. `block_threshold`) that stories A/B must add to each persisted finding record, specify it in the sidecar/finding schema (even as an optional float in `plan_review_verdict.schema.json`'s finding items), and add a fallback rendering rule (e.g. hide tick or show at 0.95 default) for pre-A/B historical sidecars that lack the field.
findings.finding: `block_threshold` is not currently persisted into the finding record by `pass3_over_findings`: the function returns `{**f, **d, 'verification': ..., 'tier': 'LLM'}` where `d` contains only `{decision, reason, validity, impact, priority, severity}` — the `block_threshold` variable is consumed locally and discarded. The plan asserts 'the value Pass-3 actually used is persisted PER FINDING by stories A/B sidecars, so the server reads it directly off the finding record,' but neither the field name, the schema change, nor the stories A/B sidecar contract are specified in this plan. The AC oracle seeds 'two findings with KNOWN, DIFFERENT per-finding `block_threshold` values on their records' — impossible without a defined field.
findings.location: What > threshold display section; AC line 4 (threshold meter oracle); src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py:382-391
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet under the threshold display section: 'Stories A/B MUST persist `block_threshold: float` on each finding record (appended by `pass3_over_findings` alongside `decision`/`priority`). The template reads `finding.block_threshold`; if absent (old sidecar), the tick is omitted and a `(threshold unavailable)` caption shown.' Update `pass3_over_findings` return line to `{**f, **d, "block_threshold": block_threshold, "verification": ..., "tier": "LLM"}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the visual system or scope that the convergence visualization uses no CSS/SVG transitions by default (static rendering), or that a `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` rule disables all transitions — and add this to AC6's oracle.
findings.finding: The convergence visualization (stacked bars / line graph) involves rendered graphical elements but the plan makes no mention of `prefers-reduced-motion`. If the SVG/CSS bars or line graph include transitions or animations, users with vestibular disorders will be affected, violating WCAG 2.3.3 (AAA) and best-practice AA guidance.
findings.location: What section (convergence visualization); Approach rationale ('inline SVG/CSS'); Acceptance Criteria AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the visual system: 'Convergence bars and line graph render statically (no CSS transitions or SVG animations); if any are added, they MUST respect `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare `jinja2` as a dependency in `pyproject.toml` (either add it to the `reviewbot` extra or confirm `fastapi[standard]` is already being used, which pulls in `starlette[full]` which pulls in Jinja2), and note this in the plan's Scope or Compatibility section.
findings.finding: The plan uses Jinja2 server-side templating but `jinja2` is not declared in `pyproject.toml` under any extra. The `reviewbot` extra declares `fastapi` (plain, without `[standard]`), and `fastapi`'s Jinja2 integration (`fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates`) requires `jinja2` as a separate install. The plan does not specify which extra the audit server belongs to, how Jinja2 gets pulled in, or whether a new `audit` extra is introduced — leaving the dependency unresolved and potentially causing an `ImportError` on a plain `pip install 'nava-rebar[reviewbot]'`.
findings.location: Scope section — 'New `src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html` (Jinja2)'; `pyproject.toml` `[project.optional-dependencies]` reviewbot entry
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"jinja2>=3"` to the `reviewbot` extra in `pyproject.toml`, or switch the `reviewbot` extra's `fastapi` entry to `fastapi[standard]` (which transitively pulls in Jinja2 via Starlette). Either way, update the plan's Scope to name the dependency change explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare Jinja2 as a runtime dependency (specify which pyproject.toml extra carries it, e.g. a new `[audit]` optional extra or the existing `[reviewbot]`/`[dev]` lists), or justify using the established stdlib f-string pattern instead by showing Jinja2's auto-escaping or template-inheritance benefits are load-bearing for this page.
findings.finding: The plan names 'Server-rendered Jinja2' as the templating engine, but Jinja2 appears nowhere in pyproject.toml (not in core deps, not in any optional extra, not in dev). The existing codebase pattern for HTML generation is stdlib f-strings (build_host_html in editor.py). Introducing Jinja2 is a novel dependency — it fires the 'unfamiliar dependency' bright line — and the plan provides no prior-art consideration of whether the existing f-string pattern suffices, nor any statement of which extra/dep entry will carry Jinja2.
findings.location: Scope section — 'New src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2)'; Approach rationale — 'Server-rendered Jinja2 keeps the page a pure function of the read-layer data'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `jinja2>=3` to a new `audit = ["jinja2>=3"]` optional-dependencies entry in pyproject.toml (mirroring the `reviewbot = ["fastapi", ...]` pattern), and reference it in the Scope section. Alternatively, if auto-escaping is the only driver, note that the page is read-only server-rendered from structured data (no user input reflected), which may make stdlib f-strings + manual html.escape() sufficient — and drop Jinja2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `criteria/model.py` exports `threshold_for()` and that the finding records persisted by stories A/B sidecars carry a `block_threshold` field (or document the exact field name in the epic-level Data Contract so the template author has a confirmed surface to bind to).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `threshold_for(finding.criteria)` exists in `criteria/model.py` and that `block_threshold` is persisted per-finding by stories A/B sidecars — meaning the server reads it 'directly off the finding record' without a runtime lookup. Neither the existence of `threshold_for()` in `criteria/model.py` nor the presence of a `block_threshold` field on sidecar finding records is verified against the actual codebase or a confirmed data contract. If this field is absent or named differently, the threshold meter AC is unachievable without schema changes.
findings.location: What section — threshold meter paragraph; AC line 4 (threshold meter oracle)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line entry to the epic's Data Contract subsection pinning the exact field name (e.g., `block_threshold: float`) on the sidecar finding record, mirroring how `criteria[]` is already pinned for story C. Confirm `threshold_for` is exported from `criteria/model.py` or remove the reference if it is unused at serve time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Inline or directly link the story C `criteria[]` field contract (field names, types, and the `kind` enum vocabulary) in this plan's Scope or Compatibility section, rather than deferring entirely to an unlinked parent-epic 'Data contract' subsection.
findings.finding: The completion panel's `criteria[]` field (from story C) is referenced with a shape `{criterion, met, citation, kind}` that does not exist in the current `completion_verdict.schema.json` or any other schema in the codebase. The plan calls this a 'hard cross-epic dependency' but does not define the field vocabulary (field names, types, nullability of `citation`, the `met` boolean, the `kind` enum values as they appear in the array) anywhere that an implementer of the template can rely on. The 'Data contract' subsection is referenced as 'pinned in the epic-level Data contract interface' but that document is not included in or linked from this plan.
findings.location: What > Completion section; Scope > templates/*.html completion-panel partial; AC line 6 (completion panel oracle)

findings.finding: The plan treats `block_threshold` as a single round-level scalar — stating 'the round's own `block_threshold`' in trust signals and 'a tick at the round's `block_threshold`' in the threshold meter description. The actual codebase shows `block_threshold` is per-criterion: `decide.py` line 16 states 'per-criterion block_threshold (parameterized)'; `criteria/model.py` lines 55-72 show `threshold_for()` returns the MIN over a finding's criteria thresholds, and `criteria_routing.json` lists 10+ distinct thresholds (0.5, 0.54, 0.6, 0.7, 0.75, 0.95). There is no single round-level block_threshold in the data model.
findings.location: What section: threshold meter description ('the round's `block_threshold` as a tick mark'); Trust signals: 'the round's own `block_threshold`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the round's `block_threshold`' with 'the finding's resolved `block_threshold` (from `threshold_for(finding.criteria)`)' in both the meter description and trust signals. AC-4's oracle should seed findings with known criteria and assert the tick reflects the per-finding resolved threshold.

findings.finding: The plan treats `block_threshold` as a single 'round-level' scalar ('the round's `block_threshold` as a tick mark'), but the actual codebase resolves `block_threshold` **per-finding** as the MIN over each finding's criteria list — and these values vary widely (0.54–0.95) across criteria. The sidecar's `_slim()` function also does not persist `block_threshold` per-finding, so the read layer has no source for the correct tick value without re-deriving it from `criteria_routing.json`. A single round-level tick would be misplaced for any finding whose effective threshold differs from the assumed modal value, actively misleading the gate-debugger audience the plan targets.
findings.location: What section — 'Threshold display = a neutral meter per finding'; AC #4; Approach rationale — 'neutral threshold meter'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and AC #4, replace 'the round's `block_threshold`' with 'the finding's effective `block_threshold`' (the value Pass-3 actually used for this finding). Add to the Scope section that the sidecar's `_slim()` must be extended to persist `block_threshold` per finding (a one-field additive change), so the read layer has the value without coupling the server to `criteria_routing.json`.

findings.finding: The completion panel 'per-criterion row' cannot be built from the completion_verdict schema: that schema only emits findings for FAILING criteria (findings[] is empty on PASS), with no field carrying the full criterion list or met/not-met status for passing criteria. The plan specifies 'one row per criterion — met/not-met glyph + criterion + kind badge + citation' but does not define what read-layer field or protocol surfaces the passing criteria so the template can render their 'met' rows.
findings.location: What > Completion section (criterion-centric, single-pass); completion_verdict.schema.json

findings.finding: The 'kind badge (codebase-verifiable / operator-attested)' referenced for each completion criterion row has no corresponding schema field in completion_verdict findings. The completion_verdict schema uses additionalProperties:true but the verifier prompt emits only 'criterion', 'detail', 'citations', 'severity', and optionally 'remediation' — no 'kind' field is specified or emitted. The rendering template has no defined field to read for the badge.
findings.location: What > Completion section; completion_verdict.schema.json; completion_verifier.md

findings.finding: The threshold meter tick is described as 'the round's block_threshold', implying a single per-round scalar. But block_threshold is per-criterion in the codebase (each criterion entry in criteria_routing.json has its own block_threshold, ranging from 0.5 to 0.95). The plan does not specify the resolution rule: use the finding's own criterion-resolved threshold (requires looking up criteria_routing.json at render time), use the minimum across the finding's criteria, or use a per-round default. This is an ambiguous processing-protocol placement.
findings.location: What > threshold display = a neutral meter; decide.py; criteria_routing.json
findings.suggested_fix: Stamp each finding in the read layer with its resolved `block_threshold` (already computed by `threshold_for()` during Pass-3) so the template can read it directly without a runtime criteria_routing lookup.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
